turns.content: I got this error when running my test and I am so frustrated because I have never programmed before but I need to pass an interview: error: ui test failed at line 10 col 23 expected "hello world" but was "world hello". What went wrong?

Don't panic, that specific error message tells us exactly what the compiler is complaining about in your UI test. The error says the macro output you captured does not match what actually happened when the code ran. In Rust testing we use a crate called trybuild to run compile-fail tests and verify that macros produce the correct output. When you write a proc-macro test with trybuild, you are essentially telling the build system to expect a specific string from your macro expansion and compare it against reality. The mismatch happens because your snapshot of expected output is wrong or your test case does not match what the real code produces.
Okay so I just need to fix the string in my file? That sounds too easy for an interview question.

That is exactly why this topic feels tricky for beginners even though it seems simple. In Rust advanced testing we do not just check if code compiles, but also verify that macros produce consistent output across different scenarios. The crate trybuild provides a trait called ui_test which lets you write tests like 'the macro should expand to hello world' and automatically compare the actual expansion against your expected string. If you are preparing for an interview, they will want to hear about how you use trybuild to run compile-fail tests where you assert that a proc-macro generates specific error messages or output strings.
But I am still confused because I have never seen this kind of testing before in other languages like Python or JavaScript.

You are absolutely right, Rust has unique testing patterns compared to those languages. In many other languages we might run tests after the code compiles using a runtime framework, but in Rust we rely heavily on compile-time checks because the compiler itself is part of our toolchain. The trait impl TryBuild allows us to define expectations for macro output directly in test files and have the build system verify them before anything runs. This means if your proc-macro changes its behavior unexpectedly, your tests will fail immediately during compilation rather than silently passing with wrong results.
So how do I set up these tests properly? Do I need to install extra packages?

Yes, you definitely need the trybuild crate which is a popular Rust testing library specifically designed for compile-fail tests and UI tests. You add it to your Cargo.toml as a dev-dependency so that only test suites use it, not your main application binary. The key concept here is using ui_tests where you define expected macro output strings in separate files or sections of your test file and let trybuild verify them against actual expansion results.
I'm trying to write tests for my custom macro that outputs strings, but I can't get the snapshot to match. What's the best way to verify the output of a proc-macro?

For verifying macro output snapshots in Rust, you should use trybuild with its ui test feature. The key is using the ui attribute on your test function and providing an expected string via the snippet parameter that gets compiled into a file named after the test name.
That sounds promising, but I'm confused about how trybuild actually captures the macro expansion. Does it just run my code and compare text output?

No, trybuild doesn't capture runtime string output like println!; it performs compile-time checks by compiling your test snippet and comparing the compiler's error messages against what you expect. The macro expansion happens during compilation so you can assert that the generated code matches exactly.
I see, but I'm still stuck on how to structure my test function properly with trybuild and ui tests.

Your test function needs the #[test] attribute along with #[cfg_attr(debug_assertions, ignore)] for debugging if needed. Inside, you declare the macro call using the snippet syntax like macro_name!(input) and then add #![expect("error message")] or #![should_panic] depending on whether your macro produces errors.
Wait a minute, I thought ui tests only check for expected output strings, but trybuild actually checks compile-time errors. How does that work together?

You're right to question this because it's easy to get confused. The ui test feature in trybuild specifically runs the code and captures compiler output, but only when using #[expect] or #[should_panic]. If your macro produces valid Rust code, you'll need a different approach like snapshot testing for runtime strings.
But my macro does produce valid code that I want to assert the output of at runtime. Is trybuild even useful then?

Trybuild is primarily for compile-time assertions, not runtime string verification. For your use case where you need to check macro expansion results at runtime, you should look into tools like insta which supports snapshot testing directly without needing compilation errors.
You're right about that limitation but I still want to understand how trybuild can be extended beyond just compile-fail tests. Are there other ways it helps with macro verification?

Trybuild can verify compile-time behavior through different angles like checking if a macro expansion compiles without errors or testing that specific error messages are generated when invalid inputs are provided to your macro.
I'm pushing back on this because I need runtime string verification, not compile-time checks. Is there any way trybuild can actually help with my specific use case?

In your specific scenario where you need to verify runtime macro output strings, trybuild alone won't solve it since it only validates compilation results. You would need to combine trybuild for compile-time checks with a separate snapshot testing framework like insta or proptest for the actual string verification you described.

I've been reviewing the new macro crate we are building, and I keep thinking about how this relates to the old compile-fail tests we used with `test` before. Back then, our team relied on `ui` tests which just checked stdout, but now we need something more robust for verifying actual compiler error messages.

That is exactly why we moved away from simple stdout checks toward using the `trybuild` crate. The core difference is that `test` only captured output text, whereas `trybuild` integrates directly with the `compiler_errors::EmitError` trait to parse and inspect the actual error structure returned by the compiler. This allows us to verify specific diagnostic messages rather than just string matching.

So instead of relying on regex patterns against stdout like we did in our old `ui` tests, `trybuild` actually parses the AST or the error tree? I want to make sure we aren't just duplicating effort with manual parsing logic.

You have hit the key distinction. While some older testing frameworks manually parsed strings, `trybuild` leverages the Rust compiler's internal structures when available or provides a much cleaner abstraction layer over those internals. It avoids reinventing the wheel by wrapping the compilation process so you can write assertions against specific error codes and messages without needing to write custom parser logic yourself.

I see, but I'm still worried about false positives if we only check for certain keywords in the error output. How does `trybuild` ensure that our snapshot tests are actually verifying the macro expansion behavior correctly? Is it just string matching again?

That is a crucial point you raised regarding false positives. Unlike simple stdout checks which can match any text, `trybuild` allows you to define specific test cases where the expected failure condition must be met exactly. For example, if your macro expansion produces an error involving a specific type or syntax rule, you can assert that this exact diagnostic appears in the output. This ensures you are testing the actual macro behavior rather than just catching a generic string pattern.

I understand now, but I'm still thinking about how we transition from our old `ui` tests to using `trybuild`. Do we need to rewrite all existing test cases? Can we use `trybuild` alongside our current setup without breaking everything?

You are right to ask about migration strategy. You do not necessarily have to rewrite every single test case immediately, as `trybuild` can be adopted incrementally for new tests or specific critical paths. For existing `ui` tests that rely on string matching, you can gradually replace those with `trybuild`'s structured assertions where the logic permits it. This phased approach ensures stability while modernizing your testing infrastructure.

One more thing I'm concerned about is how we handle snapshot tests for macro output. Does `trybuild` support comparing actual macro expansion results against a golden file? Or do we still need to maintain separate files?

To be precise, `trybuild` excels at compile-fail scenarios but does not directly support full snapshot testing of successful macro expansions in the same way some other frameworks might. For snapshotting actual output, you would typically rely on your existing infrastructure or a separate tool like `insta`. However, for verifying that specific errors occur as expected during compilation failures, `trybuild` provides the robust framework we need without requiring manual file management.

Okay, so for our current code review, I should focus on ensuring all compile-fail tests use `trybuild` and not rely on string matching? And what about the traits involved in this setup? Are there any specific implementations we need to be aware of?

Exactly. For compile-fail tests involving macros, you must ensure they leverage `trybuild`'s integration with the compiler error handling mechanism rather than relying on generic string matching. This involves correctly implementing or using traits related to diagnostic output parsing so that your test harness can accurately validate specific error conditions without ambiguity.

I'm still a bit unsure about how we handle edge cases where the macro produces unexpected errors. Does `trybuild` help us catch those, or do we need extra code? Is there any risk of missing certain failure modes?

You are correct to worry about edge cases. While `trybuild` provides a solid foundation for testing error conditions, it does not automatically detect every possible failure mode without explicit test coverage. You must ensure your test suite includes diverse scenarios that cover potential macro expansion failures, such as type mismatches or syntax errors, so that the framework can catch them effectively during compilation.
One last question: Can we trust `trybuild` to handle complex macros with nested expansions? Or do we need to manually break down the test cases into smaller units for better coverage?

`trybuild` handles complex macros well, but you should still structure your tests carefully. It is beneficial to design test cases that isolate specific macro rules and verify them individually rather than relying on a single large test file. This approach ensures better maintainability and clearer failure diagnostics when issues arise.
That makes sense. So for our next code review, I should prioritize `trybuild` tests for compile-fail scenarios and ensure we have good coverage of edge cases without over-relying on string matching?

Precisely. Your focus should shift toward verifying that all compile-fail tests utilize the proper error parsing mechanisms provided by `trybuild`, ensuring robustness against unexpected macro behaviors while maintaining a clear, maintainable test suite structure.

I'm trying to port my frontend JavaScript library to Rust, and I keep running into issues with how macros expand. My current approach feels fragile because I can't easily verify that the macro output is exactly what I expect without writing tons of manual assertions. Is there a better way to test proc-macro expansions in Rust?

You're dealing with the classic problem where macro expansion behavior changes slightly between versions, breaking your tests. The standard solution in the Rust ecosystem is using trybuild for compile-fail tests combined with ui tests. You can write snapshot tests that compare the actual output of your macro against a known-good baseline stored in a file.

I see, so I would use trybuild to check if my code compiles? But how do I actually capture the expanded macro output for comparison? The documentation mentions ui tests but I'm not sure how they work exactly with trybuild.

In trybuild, you write a test function that calls assert_fail or assert_compile_fail on your target module. For capturing expansion output specifically, you use the ui feature of trybuild which lets you pass a string parameter to the macro invocation itself. You can then compare the expanded result against a snapshot file using the expect or snap functions.

That makes sense for passing strings directly, but what if my macro is complex and generates code across multiple lines? How do I handle multi-line snapshots in trybuild? Are there specific traits or methods I need to implement on my custom error types?

For multi-line snapshots, you simply write the expected output as a string literal containing newlines directly into your test function's parameter. There are no special traits to implement beyond the standard TryBuildError trait which trybuild handles internally. You can also use the snap! macro from the snap crate if you want more flexible snapshot comparison logic.

The snap crate sounds interesting, but I'm mostly familiar with basic unit tests. Can I still rely on just the built-in trybuild UI testing without adding extra dependencies? And what happens if my actual expansion doesn't match the expected output exactly in terms of whitespace or formatting?

Yes, you can definitely use just trybuild's ui feature without extra dependencies for basic cases. If your expansions differ slightly in whitespace or formatting, trybuild will fail the test with a specific error message showing both the actual and expected output side-by-side. This helps you identify exactly what changed so you can update your snapshot file.

Okay, that's helpful. So I would create a test function that calls assert_fail on my macro module, passing in the expected string? But wait, shouldn't I be using the normal assertion helpers like assert_eq for these comparisons instead of the fail helpers? Or is there something specific about how trybuild handles this?

For compile-fail tests specifically, you should use assert_fail because that's what tells trybuild to expect a compilation failure. For regular ui tests where you want to check expansion output without expecting failures, you'd use the regular assertion helpers like assert_eq or assert_matches within your test function context.

I'm confused about when to use which approach. Let me clarify: if my macro is supposed to always succeed and I just want to verify its output matches expectations, do I need a special flag in trybuild? Or can I mix regular tests with ui testing seamlessly?

You don't need any special flags for successful macros. You can write standard unit tests alongside your ui tests using the same test module structure. Just ensure you're calling the right assertion helper based on whether you expect success or failure from that particular test case.

Alright, I think I understand the pattern now. One last question: what happens if my macro implementation changes and produces slightly different output? Does trybuild automatically detect this as a regression, or do I need to manually update the snapshot files every time? How does it handle versioning of these test expectations?

When your macro output changes slightly, trybuild will fail the test with clear error messages showing the differences between actual and expected. This acts like an automatic regression detector for you. You can then use tools like git to track snapshot file changes or manually update them once you've verified the new behavior is correct.

That's exactly what I needed! So my aha moment is that trybuild gives me immediate feedback on macro output drift without needing complex runtime testing frameworks? I was overthinking this by trying to implement manual string comparisons instead of leveraging the built-in snapshot infrastructure. Thanks for guiding me through the ui test concept with trybuild.

You're welcome! The key insight is that compile-time testing with tools like trybuild catches macro expansion issues early before they reach production code, which is much more efficient than runtime verification. Your frontend-to-Rust port should benefit greatly from this approach to ensure your macros behave consistently across versions.
I'm trying to write tests for my new macro but the output keeps changing and I can't catch it. Why does this even work this way where every tiny formatting difference breaks my test suite?

That frustration is common when testing macros because their string output can vary slightly between versions or platforms. The solution is to use the trybuild crate specifically for compile-fail tests and ui tests where you capture a snapshot of macro output.
So I should be using something like assert_eq! on strings? But that feels wrong since macros produce code, not just data.

You are correct to feel that way. assert_eq! is for comparing values at runtime, but macro expansion happens at compile time so you need a tool designed for that phase. The trybuild crate provides the necessary infrastructure to handle these compile-time failures and output comparisons.
Okay, but how do I actually set this up? Do I just add it as a dependency?

Yes, you add the trybuild crate to your Cargo.toml dependencies alongside any dev-dependencies needed for testing. This allows you to write tests that verify macro expansion produces expected error messages or code patterns.
Wait, so I'm not actually running the macro? Just checking if it compiles with specific errors?

You are partially right but there is a nuance here. You can test compile-fail cases where macros intentionally produce errors, and you can also use ui tests to verify that macro expansion produces exactly the expected code snippet in the output.
Can you explain the difference between these two approaches more clearly?

Think of it like this: compile-fail tests are like checking if a door locks properly by trying to open it with the wrong key, while ui tests are like taking a photo of what the machine prints out when you run a specific command. The trybuild crate handles both scenarios automatically.
So for my macro that expands to some code, I'd use ui tests and capture the output?
Exactly. You write a test case where your macro is invoked with specific arguments, then you tell trybuild what the expected expanded output should look like using snapshot assertions.
And if I want to check that my macro generates an error message when used incorrectly?

Then you use the compile-fail test feature in trybuild. You provide a snippet of code with a deliberate syntax error or invalid usage, and assert that compilation fails with exactly the expected error message.
Ah, so I can combine both? Like one test for normal expansion and another for error cases?
Yes, you can have multiple test functions in your test module. One uses ui tests to verify successful expansions and others use compile-fail tests to ensure invalid usage produces the correct errors.
That makes sense now. It's like having different validation rules for different scenarios instead of one giant check.
Precisely. And the trybuild crate abstracts away all the complex details so you only focus on writing clear tests rather than wrestling with build systems.
What's the difference between trybuild and regular ui tests when I'm writing compile-fail tests for my proc macro?

The main distinction is that trybuild is specifically designed to test what happens when compilation fails, whereas standard ui tests usually capture successful output or behavior. When you're debugging a proc macro edge case where the code should not compile, trybuild lets you define expected error messages directly in your test files using the fail_test attribute and expect_err fields. You can also use snapshot testing with trybuild to compare the actual stderr against a baseline file, which is much easier than manually writing ui strings for every line of the error output.
Oh I see, so I just need to write some text files instead of running the compiler? Can't I just copy my existing ui tests and paste them in?

You can reuse your existing ui test structure, but there are key differences in how you configure the macro expansion context. With trybuild, you're testing compile-fail scenarios so you need to ensure your proc macro is configured to emit errors rather than succeed. You'll also want to check that your test runner actually invokes the cargo command with --test flag enabled and passes the right arguments to trigger the failure condition. The error output format might differ slightly depending on whether you're using nightly features or stable toolchain versions.
I've been reading about trybuild and ui tests but I still don't fully grasp how to actually set them up in my project without getting confused by the syntax.

It's totally normal to feel that way, even after reading the docs. The key is understanding that trybuild acts as a compile-fail test runner which expects your macro output to match a specific snapshot file you provide. You define a test function in your crate source code where you call the macro and pass it arguments, then use the ui attribute on that function to point to a .ui file containing what the compiler should error out with.
Okay so I need to write a custom test function inside my library crate instead of relying on external crates automatically? And how do I handle the actual snapshot comparison logic?

Yes, you define the test function directly in your own crate source code. The trybuild crate handles the execution and comparison behind the scenes by running the rustc compiler against your test module and checking if the stderr output matches exactly what's written in the .ui file. You don't write any manual string comparison logic yourself; instead, you just ensure the ui attribute points to the correct relative path of the snapshot file within your crate directory.

That makes sense about not writing manual comparisons but I'm still confused about how trybuild actually handles the compilation errors and what specific traits or methods it uses internally under the hood?

Under the hood, trybuild relies on the rustc compiler to execute your test function and then parses the stderr stream from that execution. It doesn't implement a custom string comparison trait; instead, it reads the raw output from the compiler process and compares it byte-for-byte against the expected content in your ui file using simple string equality checks internally within its own library implementation.
So if my macro produces slightly different error messages than what's in the .ui file, trybuild will just fail immediately without any fancy diffing or suggestions?

Exactly, that's one of the main reasons why writing these tests requires extreme precision. If your macro output changes even by a single character compared to the snapshot, the test fails instantly because trybuild performs exact string matching rather than offering a diff view like some other testing frameworks might. You must manually update the .ui file whenever you fix an error message in your macro code.
I see but I'm wondering if there's any way to make this easier since I'm used to seeing more flexible assertion messages in my Java projects where junit gives me a nice diff output?

You're right that Java frameworks like JUnit often provide nicer diffs, but trybuild was designed specifically for the Rust ecosystem where compile-fail tests are common and precise error matching is preferred over fuzzy matching. There isn't built-in support for automatic diffing in trybuild itself though some community extensions or custom wrappers might attempt to add this functionality on top of the basic mechanism.
Interesting so I should just be very careful when updating my snapshots since any typo will break everything?

Yes, absolutely. Since it's exact string matching, even a single typo in your .ui file or an unexpected whitespace difference in the macro output will cause the test to fail immediately. You'll need to double-check that every character in your snapshot matches exactly what the compiler actually outputs for your specific error case.
What about when I'm testing multiple variants of a macro with different arguments? Do I just create separate ui files for each?

Yes, you typically create one .ui file per test case where each file corresponds to a single variant of your macro. In your test function you call the macro with specific arguments and annotate it with the ui attribute pointing to that specific snapshot file. If you have many variants you can group them in separate modules or use conditional compilation macros to select different tests based on configuration flags.

You could also organize multiple test cases into a single function by using the #[test] macro attributes differently, but typically it's cleaner to keep each distinct error scenario as its own test function with its own ui file. This way you can run subsets of your tests easily without mixing unrelated expectations.
I think I understand now about separating them so what if I want to verify that my macro handles both valid and invalid inputs correctly? Should I use trybuild for the invalid ones?

Yes, trybuild is specifically designed for compile-fail tests which means it's perfect for verifying your macro rejects invalid input with appropriate error messages. For valid inputs you might want to use normal unit tests instead since those will run successfully without errors and verify that the macro compiles cleanly when given correct arguments.
What is the difference between writing compile-fail tests and using ui tests in Rust?

The main distinction lies in how you verify macro output. Compile-fail tests rely on the `trybuild` crate, which runs your code through rustc and checks if it fails to compile with specific error messages. You define a test that expects compilation to fail and then assert that the stderr matches what you specified using the `assert_fail!()` macro from trybuild. In contrast, ui tests are designed for passing code where you want to verify the output of macros or the compiler's diagnostic messages when errors occur, but with more flexibility in how those outputs are checked.
So if I have a macro that expands correctly but produces unexpected formatting, which approach should I use?

You would definitely need to use ui tests in this scenario. Since your code compiles successfully, trybuild's compile-fail feature is not applicable because it relies on the compiler rejecting the code. Instead, you should capture the macro expansion output or diagnostic messages from a successful compilation and compare them against expected strings using the `ui` test attribute.
How do I set up the environment for these tests?

You need to add trybuild as a dev dependency in your Cargo.toml file. Then create a `tests` directory alongside your main source code. Inside that, you place test files with the `.rs` extension where you define your test cases using the #[test] and #![cfg_attr(...)] attributes.
Can I run these tests separately from my normal build?

Yes, you can invoke them independently using the `cargo test` command. However, since trybuild operates at compile time, it does not actually execute your code; instead, it compiles your crate and captures its output to compare against expectations.
I'm confused about the difference between ui tests and snapshot tests in this context.

In trybuild, a ui test is essentially a snapshot test but written differently. You manually specify what the expected output should be using string literals within your test function, rather than loading a pre-existing file. This gives you more control over how the assertion is structured and allows for easier inline modifications without managing external files.
What happens if my macro output changes but I don't update the test?

The test will fail immediately when you run cargo test. The failure message will clearly indicate which line in your expected string differs from what was actually produced by the compiler, helping you identify exactly where your macro needs adjustment.
Do I need to worry about different Rust versions affecting these tests?

Yes, this is a critical consideration. Different editions or compiler versions might produce slightly different error messages or macro expansions. You should ensure your test suite covers the version ranges you intend to support and possibly use conditional compilation attributes like #[cfg(all(feature = "stable", feature = "nightly"))] to handle variations.
How do I structure a simple ui test case?

You start by writing a regular #[test] function. Inside, you use the `ui` attribute to mark it as a compile-fail or compile-pass test depending on your needs. Then you declare an expected string variable and pass it to the assert_eq macro along with the actual compiler output captured during execution.
Can I combine multiple assertions in one ui test?

Yes, you can chain multiple assert_eq calls within a single test function to verify several aspects of the macro output simultaneously. This keeps your tests concise and ensures that all expected behaviors are validated together.
What if my macro depends on external crates?

You can still write tests for these scenarios, but you may need to mock or stub those dependencies in your test environment. Alternatively, you could run the tests with a clean dependency tree by temporarily removing problematic crates before executing the test suite.
Is there any way to automate the snapshot updates?

While trybuild doesn't provide built-in automation for this, you can write a small script or use CI tools like GitHub Actions that detect test failures and automatically update your expected output files if configured appropriately.
I'm still not clear on how the compiler actually generates these outputs.

When rustc compiles your crate, it captures all diagnostic messages and macro expansions into a temporary file. trybuild reads this output and compares it against what you specified in your test function using string matching logic implemented internally within the crate.
So if I want to verify that my macro generates valid syntax?

You would use compile-pass tests. These ensure that your macro expands into syntactically correct Rust code without triggering any compiler errors. The test verifies successful compilation rather than checking for specific error messages.
What's the best practice when writing these tests?

Keep each test focused on a single aspect of your macro behavior. Use descriptive names for your test functions and ensure they are grouped logically in your test directory structure to maintain clarity and make debugging easier.
